text.title: Requirements Traceability — Process and Maintenance
text.summary: Four-step traceability process: define strategy, create matrix, maintain on change, analyze coverage. Includes the generated-not-maintained rule.

rule.statement: Decide traceability depth (minimum/standard/full) and the complete link role vocabulary before creating any artifact. Encode both in a YAML config file committed to the repo root.
rule.rationale: Strategy decisions made after artifacts exist produce inconsistent coverage; changing the strategy mid-project requires retroactive linking that agents and humans both do poorly.

section.title: Step 2: Create Traceability Matrix — Generated, Not Maintained

rule.statement: The RTM is a generated artifact, not a hand-edited file. Each source artifact carries typed links in its frontmatter (traces: [BR-05, SR-12]); a builder script aggregates them into the matrix and computes coverage. Agents emit patches to source artifact frontmatter; the generator owns the canonical matrix state.
rule.rationale: Spreadsheet RTMs go stale within 2-3 sprints because no single person owns the maintenance burden. Generated matrices stay current as long as source artifacts are edited and the generator runs in CI.

section.reference: Python script generating RTM from frontmatter traces across a docs tree

rule.statement: When a requirement is added, link it to its parent before committing. When a requirement changes, walk downstream links and flag affected artifacts. When a requirement is deleted, remove all links and update the matrix.
rule.rationale: Unlinked new requirements become orphans that fail coverage checks; unchanged downstream links after a requirement change produce misleading impact assessments.

rule.statement: Use Conventional Commits with REQ scope (feat(REQ-045): ...) and Refs: footers for multi-requirement commits to get a free code-to-req trace via git log.
rule.rationale: Structured commit messages feed backward traceability from code to requirement without any additional tooling beyond the git log.

rule.statement: Tag a traceability baseline at every release. PRs must show the coverage diff so a regression is visible in review.
rule.rationale: Baseline diffs make coverage regression visible before merge; without them, regressions accumulate silently until audit time.

antipattern.description: Agents write the RTM directly instead of patching source artifact frontmatter.
antipattern.reason: Direct RTM edits bypass the generator and produce inconsistencies between source artifacts and the matrix; the generator must own the canonical state.

antipattern.description: LLMs hallucinate links by associating artifacts that share a keyword but have no actual dependency.
antipattern.reason: Hallucinated links poison the matrix and pass coverage thresholds while hiding real gaps. Every proposed link must cite a quoted span from each artifact as evidence; links below 0.9 confidence require human review.

antipattern.description: Too granular tracing — linking every UI label and log message.
antipattern.reason: Over-tracing buries signal in noise; trace only at the lowest level where a defect would be material to the business or auditor.
